question_text: Which source backbone maps to U09 - Observation and Charting II: Documentation and Reporting?
rationale: U09 is mapped to Module 15 - Observation and Charting, with emphasis on charting documents, ADL/MDS-related documentation, reporting, and correction principles.

question_text: Resident K slides from a wheelchair to the floor, states that the arm stings, and a coworker suggests charting only assisted with transfer. What is the best CNA response?
answer_a: Chart assisted with transfer because a coworker recommended it.
answer_b: Stay with the resident as safe, report immediately to the licensed nurse, document facts, and follow incident policy.
answer_c: Move the resident back into the wheelchair before telling anyone.
answer_d: Wait until the end of the shift to see whether pain continues.
rationale: The scenario requires immediate reporting, factual documentation, and refusal to falsify the record; routine documentation does not replace notifying licensed staff.

question_text: Which action is outside CNA scope and violates legal charting principles in U09?
answer_a: Documenting care after it is provided.
answer_c: Falsifying a note to make an unusual event look like routine care.
answer_d: Reporting a change in condition to the licensed nurse.
rationale: Falsification is unsafe, unethical, and outside CNA responsibilities; CNAs document truthful facts and follow policy.

question_text: Which documentation entry is most objective for a fictional slide-to-floor event after the nurse has been notified?
answer_a: Resident was careless and caused a fall.
answer_b: Resident probably has a new injury and should not reach for things.
answer_c: Coworker said not to make a big deal out of it.
answer_d: At 0915 resident slid from wheelchair to floor while reaching toward closet; stated, 'my arm stings'; small open area observed on left forearm; nurse notified at 0916.
rationale: The correct entry records time, observed facts, the resident's own statement, the skin observation, and notification without blame or diagnosis.

question_text: Which statement best distinguishes reporting from documentation?
answer_a: Reporting is timely communication to the correct staff member; documentation is the official factual record completed according to policy.
answer_b: Documentation replaces reporting when a chart note is entered.
answer_c: Reporting is only needed if the resident asks for it.
answer_d: Documentation should include opinions so the nurse knows what the CNA thinks.
rationale: U09 emphasizes that urgent findings must be reported promptly and then documented factually; the two actions support each other but are not interchangeable.

question_text: A CNA accidentally enters a note under the wrong fictional resident in an electronic chart. What is the safest next step?
answer_a: Delete the note and say nothing if the system allows it.
answer_b: Follow facility policy for electronic correction or amendment, notify the appropriate supervisor if required, and enter the correct note in the correct record.
answer_c: Ask a coworker to chart a replacement note under the coworker's login.
answer_d: Backdate the corrected note so the time looks unchanged.
rationale: The correction process should preserve the record, identify and correct the error through approved channels, and avoid deletion, backdating, or another person's login.

question_text: A resident usually needs supervision for toileting but today required weight-bearing help and refused a shower. Which documentation choice best supports ADL/MDS-related accuracy?
answer_a: Chart the usual level of supervision because that is the resident's baseline.
answer_b: Chart total dependence for all ADLs because the day was difficult.
answer_c: Document the actual assistance provided, the shower refusal, alternatives offered as assigned, and report the change according to policy.
answer_d: Leave the ADL fields blank because the MDS team will decide later.
rationale: ADL documentation should reflect actual resident performance and staff support for the time period, including refusals and reportable changes.

question_text: Which pairing best describes common documentation documents used in CNA work?
answer_a: Incident reports are used to replace nurse notification.
answer_b: Meal intake records are used to diagnose swallowing disorders.
answer_c: Care plans are where CNAs change therapy orders independently.
answer_d: ADL flow sheets record assistance provided, and incident reports record facts about unusual events according to policy.
rationale: U09 focuses on using the right document for the right purpose while staying within CNA scope and facility policy.

question_text: At 1400, a CNA realizes an 1100 report to the nurse about a skin tear was not documented. What is the best action?
answer_a: Follow facility late-entry policy with the current date/time, the actual event time, factual details, and the nurse notification information.
answer_b: Backdate the entry to 1100 so no one sees it was late.
answer_c: Skip the entry because the nurse already knows.
answer_d: Ask another aide to write the note instead.
rationale: A late entry should be transparent, factual, and completed according to policy; backdating or omitting the event creates an inaccurate record.

question_text: In a Moodle documentation practice activity, which action best protects confidentiality?
answer_a: Use real resident initials but omit the last name.
answer_b: Use only the fictional scenario details provided in the course and no real facility or resident information.
answer_c: Use a real event from work because it improves realism.
answer_d: Upload a screenshot of an electronic chart with identifiers covered by a sticky note.
rationale: The course requires fictional, de-identified practice only; real resident or facility information and EHR screenshots must not be entered into Moodle.
